    Appendix: Greenfield Projects

    A common question comes up again and again when building projects: what's the difference between greenfield and brownfield development?

    Greenfield is when you're starting a new project where there is nothing there before you. The field is green, it has not been built on yet.

    Brownfield means the field has something on it already. You're building in an existing setup, perhaps a legacy codebase that needs to integrate with what's already there. There's stuff on the ground already that impacts what you're doing.

    Most of the work we've done in this cohort has been brownfield. We've given you an existing application and worked within its constraints, using preexisting patterns in the codebase.

    But how do you start when there's nothing to start from? How do you start from a blank page?

    Start With a Grilling Session

    The first thing to start with is a grilling session. You have some kind of vague idea that needs hammering out, and you chat with an agent about it.

    This is where you capture all your initial thinking and rough ideas about what you're going to build.

    Build Your Research Documentation

    From the grilling session, save everything into research files. Anything captured during those grilling sessions should be saved in the early phases as markdown documents in the repo itself.

    The idea is to have multiple grilling sessions, continually refining your idea for what you're trying to build. Save the output of those into markdown documents so you build up a codebase - but of markdown documents - with all the decisions you've made.

    This creates a living document of your project's evolution and reasoning.

    Prototype Your Ideas

    Once you have some research, start prototyping. Take some of the research questions you had and turn them into prototypes.

    These prototypes might get reused later, or they might just get saved in the codebase as reference material. Continue to iterate on them so you start to get a clearer picture of what you actually want to build.

    Write Your Product Requirements Document

    Once you have that clear picture, it's time to write the product requirements document. This will look a little different in a greenfield codebase compared to a brownfield codebase.

    Understanding UX and AX

    To understand why, we need to talk about two pieces of terminology: UX and AX.

    TermMeaningFocus
    UXUser experienceThe experience of the end user
    AXAgent experienceThe experience of the agent working in the codebase

    In a brownfield codebase, we usually have all the fundamentals nailed down and can focus on the user. But in a greenfield codebase, the first thing we need to think about is the AX - the agent experience.

    This means your first product requirements document needs to specify some UX with end-facing user experience goals. But you also need to focus heavily on agent experience, with a focus on feedback loops, the languages you're using, and the technologies you're using.

    Agent Experience Essentials

    Here are the key areas to focus on when designing agent experience:

    Feedback Loops

    Establish types, tests, and pre-commit hooks, as well as formatting tools that make sure the agent is producing high-quality code from day one.

    Technology Choices

    These are often really difficult decisions that are difficult to swap out of later. Figure out:

    • What language you're going to use
    • What front-end framework (if building a front-end app)
    • These choices should be thoroughly grilled on with the agent

    Module Shape

    Think about the initial shape of the modules and the deep modules you're going to create. Code-based architecture is crucial to agent experience, so get this right early.

    Testing Strategies

    This ties directly into module shape. Decide what things you're going to mock, whether you'll use a test database, and similar concerns. Quality code from the start depends on these choices.

    The AX-First Approach

    Your first PRD is crucial because you probably need a PRD for the AX before you even look at the UX.

    What you're building will influence your technology choice, will influence your testing strategy, and will definitely influence your module shape. You can't sacrifice AX for the sake of UX - they need to be tied together. The thing you're building needs to feed into the way you build it.

    Back to Brownfield

    Once you've got your AX sorted (often via its own PRD), then you can start going through implementing issues, grabbing them, doing reviews - and you're back into essentially a brownfield codebase.

    The difficulty of setting up a greenfield codebase is really just setting up the agent experience. Once that's done, you can immediately start going through the loop we've talked about throughout this course.

    If you have any more questions, ask them in the Discord. This is a particularly juicy topic to grill on together.
